method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
No source documents were provided, so I can’t write a cited “Flora and fauna” section using the required document-number citation format.

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent inhabitants, and consequently has no resident workforce, domestic market, or settlement-based local economy.[1] Historical economic interest centered on guano deposits: the United States claimed Howland, along with Baker Island, under the Guano Islands Act in 1857, but the deposits are now exhausted.[1] In the 1930s, the island also had a limited transport role as a planned stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[1] Modern economic activity is minimal, with the island administered as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and included within the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[1] In regional fisheries and ocean-use reporting, Howland is grouped with other Pacific Remote Island Areas within U.S. Pacific waters; that grouping accounted for 2 percent of the referenced deployments in one Hawaii-based fisheries analysis.[3]

==Time zone==
Howland Island is in the UTC−12:00 time zone, designated “Yankee” in nautical time-zone notation, which it shares with nearby Baker Island.[2] The island has no permanent or Indigenous population, and public entry is permitted only by special permit.[4] Because UTC−12:00 is uninhabited, Howland and Baker are commonly cited as the last places on Earth where a calendar date remains in effect.[3][5] The “Anywhere on Earth” deadline convention therefore treats a date as expiring when it has ended on Howland and Baker Islands.[3] When uninhabited UTC−12:00 islands are excluded, UTC−11:00 is generally treated as the last inhabited time zone.[3][5]

==History==
No archaeological evidence has been found for an early Polynesian presence on Howland Island.[4] The first recorded sighting was in 1822 by George Worth, a Nantucket whaling-ship captain, who named it Worth Island.[2] In 1842, Captain George E. Netcher of the New Bedford whaleship *Isabella* visited the island and gave it the name Howland Island.[4]

The United States claimed Howland in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act of 1856, at about the same time it claimed nearby Baker Island.[2] Guano extraction began in the late 1850s, and competing American companies later disputed control of the deposits before a court settlement in 1865 allowed both to mine on the island.[4] The guano-mining enterprises ended in 1878 after the deposits were depleted.[4]

In 1935, colonists associated with the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panalaʻau Project, arrived from Hawaii to establish a permanent United States presence in the central Pacific.[4] The settlement they founded was named Itascatown after the U.S. Coast Guard Cutter *Itasca*.[4] Howland was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936 and was intended to serve as a stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[2]

On July 2, 1937, Amelia Earhart and navigator Fred Noonan disappeared near Howland Island while attempting to reach it from Lae, New Guinea, during Earhart’s round-the-world flight.[2] During World War II, the island was used as a lookout point by the United States military.[1] In later decades, Howland’s human use shifted away from settlement and extraction toward conservation, and it is now managed as part of the United States National Wildlife Refuge system.[3]

==Earhart Light==
Earhart Light is a small day beacon on Howland Island, originally built in 1937 as part of preparations for Amelia Earhart’s planned arrival at the island.[3] The structure was intended to aid navigation but never served its planned role in Earhart’s flight.[1][3] During World War II, Japanese attacks damaged the beacon and rendered it unusable; one account notes that a plaque honoring Earhart was shattered by machine-gun fire.[2][5] The station has been inactive since 1942, and the surviving structure is regarded as a day beacon rather than an operational lighthouse.[3][5] The lighthouse, now commonly known as Earhart Light, was partially destroyed during the war and rebuilt in the early 1960s.[4] Later visits to the island included restoration work and the addition of the names of two colonists killed during the wartime attack.[2]

==Image gallery==
A map in the gallery identifies Howland Island and provides a geographic overview of the island.[1]

An astronaut photograph taken during ISS Expedition 10 on 3 December 2004 shows Howland Island from orbit.[2][3]

The ISS image, catalogued as frame ISS010-E-09287, was taken with a Kodak DCS760C electronic still camera using an 800 mm lens.[2][3][4]

In the orbital image, white breakers encircling the island mark the surrounding fringing reef.[2][4]

NASA notes that astronaut photographs of reefs and islands are used for mapping, reef-ecosystem assessment, and bathymetric studies.[2][4]

The NASA image is in the public domain in the United States because it was created by the Image Science & Analysis Laboratory at NASA’s Johnson Space Center.[3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
The Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974, with refuge jurisdiction originally including the island, surrounding waters, and submerged and emergent lands from the mean low tide line out to 3 nautical miles.[4] The refuge protects nesting and foraging habitat for seabirds and other marine wildlife, including migratory seabirds, shorebirds, and threatened or endangered sea turtles.[1][3] In 2009, Howland was included within the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, and the refuge boundary was extended to 12 nautical miles as part of that designation.[4] Monument protections around Howland extend to 50 nautical miles, with waters and submerged and emergent lands managed cooperatively by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.[4] Refuge planning has been conducted under the National Wildlife Refuge System Administration Act, as amended by the National Wildlife Refuge System Improvement Act of 1997, and the National Environmental Policy Act.[5] Management priorities described for the refuge system include more frequent biological surveys, continued scientific data collection, habitat-condition assessment, additional marine-habitat exploration, efforts to attract rare seabird species, and expanded research opportunities.[5] No public uses of the refuge were proposed in the cited management planning documents.[5]
